coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis and enhanced microwave absorption properities of novel lightweight graphene quantum dots/hexagonal boron nitride composites
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qi
Fang, Yi
Du, Zhao
Guo, Zhonglu
Liu, Zhenya
Huang, Yang
Lin, Jing
Tang, Chengchun
coredata.dc:description: Here we report the successful preparation of hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets supported graphene quantum dots (GQDs/BNNs) composites by one-step hydrothermal method. For the first time, GQDs was used as an absorbent for microwave absorbing materials. In this work, the relationship between the concentration of GQDs in GQDs/BNNs composites and the microwave absorption performance was discussed. As an efficient wave absorber, GQDs/BNNs had excellent microwave absorption properties. In the frequency bands of 2–18 GHz, minimum reflection loss (RL min) of GQDs/BNNs can reach as low as −59.9 dB at 2.8 mm. Significantly, effective bandwidth of RL values exceeding −10 dB was about 35 GHz, resulting in covering almost entire measured bandwidth. Furthermore, BNNs with high stability, thermal conductivity and hydrophobicity can greatly enhance the overall performance of GQDs/BNNs. Finally, GQDs/BNNs composites with strong absorption and wide frequency will be a novel promising absorbent in the field of microwave absorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Boron nitride
One-step hydrothermal method
Microwave absorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic scheme for the preparation of GQDs/BNNs composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM, (b) HRTEM, (c) AFM and (d) photoluminescent spectrum of GQDs (314 nm) (e) GQDs under natural light (1) and ultraviolet light at 365 nm (2). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(A) XPS survey spectra of BNNs and GQDs/BNNs (S9), core level spectra of (b) B 1s, (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s in S9. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of (a, b) S1, (c) S3, (d) S9 samples and (e–f) elemental mappings of B, C and N in S9. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra of (a) BNNs, GQDs and S9, (b) two-dimensional and (c) three-dimensional representation of RL curves for GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Three-dimensional (left) and two-dimensional (right) representation of RL curves for the (a) BNNs, (b–f) S1, S3, S5, S7 and S9 with a certain thickness in the frequency range of 2.0–18.0 GHz. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Frequency dependence of (a) ε′, (b) ε″, (c) μ′, (d) μ″, (e) tanδ
E and (f) tanδ
M for BNNs, GQDs and S1∼S9. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The contrast patterns of (a) α and (b) impedance matching ratio for BNNs, GQDs and S1∼S9. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The RL curves for BNNs, S1∼S9 in the frequency range of (a) 19–26 and (b) 27–40 GHz. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram of microwave absorption mechanism for GQDs/BNNs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Microwave absorption performance values of this work and related references.

head.ce:title: Controllable synthesis and enhanced microwave absorption properities of novel lightweight graphene quantum dots/hexagonal boron nitride composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Boron Nitride Micro and Nano Materials, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Boron Nitride Micro and Nano Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Key Laboratory of Boron Nitride Micro and Nano Materials, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China.
Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we report the successful preparation of hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets supported graphene quantum dots (GQDs/BNNs) composites by one-step hydrothermal method. For the first time, GQDs was used as an absorbent for microwave absorbing materials. In this work, the relationship between the concentration of GQDs in GQDs/BNNs composites and the microwave absorption performance was discussed. As an efficient wave absorber, GQDs/BNNs had excellent microwave absorption properties. In the frequency bands of 2–18 GHz, minimum reflection loss (RL min) of GQDs/BNNs can reach as low as −59.9 dB at 2.8 mm. Significantly, effective bandwidth of RL values exceeding −10 dB was about 35 GHz, resulting in covering almost entire measured bandwidth. Furthermore, BNNs with high stability, thermal conductivity and hydrophobicity can greatly enhance the overall performance of GQDs/BNNs. Finally, GQDs/BNNs composites with strong absorption and wide frequency will be a novel promising absorbent in the field of microwave absorption.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advent of the electronic information age, solving electromagnetic (EM) interference and pollution problems caused by EM radiation has attracted universal attention. Many researchers have made various attempts to develop an ideal microwave absorption material with the advantages of efficient, lightweight, corrosion-resistant and easy-to-fabricate [1]. Generally speaking, the most common microwave absorption materials are mainly consist of magnetic loss material and dielectric loss materials [2,3]. In recent years, one of the common methods is to combine lightweight carbon-based materials (graphene, carbon nanotubes and carbon fiber) and metallic magnetic materials (iron and ferrites) to achieve high microwave absorption properties by adjusting complex permittivity and permeability [4–6]. Dai et al. prepared magnetic aligned Fe3O4-reduced graphene oxide (FRGO)/waterborne polyurethaned (WPU) composites. The optimum microwave absorption performance was obtained for FRGO/WPU composites, and a RL min of −67.8 dB was achieved at 13.6 GHz for a thickness of 2.4 mm [7]. Zhang et al. implanted small NiFe2O4 clusters on reduced graphene oxide (rGO). The NiFe2O4-rGO (2.7 mm) has the RL min of −58 dB, and the effective bandwidth is from 4.6 to 16 GHz [8].

Unfortunately, graphene is a semiconductor with zero band gap, and it does not have excellent microwave absorption capability by itself. Due to the large dielectric constant of graphene, it is easy to cause strong reflection when electromagnetic waves touch its surface [9]. However, GQDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials formed by cutting graphene and confining it in a small area. It have a wide and continuous absorption band in the ultraviolet region, and the absorption is strong. The transition from the π orbital of the CC bond to the π∗ orbital of the GQDs structure column, or the transition from the n orbital to the π∗ orbital of the CO bond can cause characteristic peaks. Therefore, using and maintaining the excellent electron mobility of GQDs is the key to studying its microwave absorption properties. Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) has excellent properties such as high melting point, high thermal conductivity, stable chemical properties and corrosion resistance. In recent years, there have been some studies in the field of EM wave absorption by combining it with traditional wave absorbing materials to achieve the enhancement of impedance matching rate and the optimization of stable performance. The general method is to use BN as an outer layer material to cover the absorbing material or form a sandwich structure to exert the wave-transmitting properties. Zhou et al. synthesized BN coatings on pyrolytic carbon (BN-coated PyC) particles by boric acid and urea in nitrogen. It shows a strong absorbing peak at 10.64 GHz, and the lowest reflectivity can be reached to −21.72 dB [10]. Kang et al. prepared rGO-hBN sandwichlike hybrid materials by GO and ammonia borane (AB). When the mass ratio of GO/AB is 1:1, the microwave absorption of the hybrid treated at 900 °C is preferable in the range of 6–18 GHz. A RL min of −40.5 dB was observed at 15.3 GHz for the composite filled with 25 wt % rGO-hBN at the thickness of 1.6 mm [11].

In this contribution, we have designed and prepared a kind of novel lightweight GQDs/BNNs composites. The typical synthetic strategy is shown in Fig. 1 . The effects on the permittivity and permeability were investigated by loading different concentrations of GQDs on the surface of BNNs. Furthermore, the microwave absorption properties such as RL, attenuation constant (α) and impedance matching ratio of GQDs/BNNs has been measured, showing excellent microwave absorption intensity and widen effective frequency band. Meanwhile, the mechanism of improved microwave absorption properties has been discussed in detail.

Boron acid (H3BO3) and urea (CO(NH2)2) were supplied by Aladdin Industrial Inc. Graphite powder, sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), nitric acid (HNO3, 69%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4) were purchased in Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals are analytically pure without further purification.
BNNs was prepared to use boron acid (H3BO3) and urea (CO(NH2)2) as the starting materials and annealed at 950 °C under a cover of N2 atmosphere [12].

The graphite powder was uniformly mixed with KMnO4 in proportion, and then H2SO4 and HNO3 were added. The mixture was stirred for 24 h to give a brownish red viscous material. After cooling by an ice bath, H2O2 was added to obtain a brownish-yellow suspension. The obtained product was washed with HCl and deionized water several times, and then was freeze-dried to obtain GO [13].

GQDs was prepared by “top-down approach”: 0.05 g GO and 0.4 g NaOH were ultrasonically dispersed in 80 mL of deionized water for 1 h. The mixture transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept at 200 °C. The product was separated by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter to give a pale yellow solution. The solution was dialyzed to pH = 7 and collected to obtain GQDs [14]. The preparation process of GQDs/BNNs is as follows: Firstly, the prepared GQDs were mixed with BNNs into the Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. Then, 80 ml of deionized water was added and kept at 140 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, a brownish white powder was obtained by centrifugation and dried at 60 ° C. The samples were prepared for different concentrations of BNNs and GQDs (molar ratio = 1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 1:7, 1:9), which were defined as S1, S3, S5, S7 and S9, respectively.

The surface of the sample was characterized using an atomic force microscope (AFM, Agilent, A5500). The morphology of the sample was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Talos F200S). The Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy to analyze the chemical bond type of the samples (FT-IR, BRUKER, TENSOR 27). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to analyze the type of bonding of the samples (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). The photoluminescence spectrum spectra of the samples are recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, BRUKER, V80). The complex permittivity and permeability values were measured in the 2–18, 19–26 and 27–40 GHz range with coaxial wire and waveguide method by AV3672C PNA-L Network Analyzer. The tested samples were uniformly mixed with paraffin and placed in a mold (GQDs/BNNs 0.12 g, paraffin 0.15 g). The mixture was pressed into toroidal-shaped samples with an inner diameter of 3.00 mm and an outer diameter of 7.00 mm.

The morphology and size distribution of the obtained GQDs are displayed in Fig. 2 a-c. The images of TEM and AFM reveal that the average sizes of GQDs are approximately 2–5 nm and the interplanar spacing of ordered lattice is around 0.24 nm [15]. A strong broad emission band from 470 to 650 nm in the yellow-green region, peaking at 533.8 nm, can be observed when GQDs is excited at 450 nm (Fig. 2d). As revealed by Fig. 2e, a light yellow-green emission color can be observed under a UV lamp of 365 nm, which matches the corresponding emission spectrum [16]. According to the results, GQDs with uniform size and shape were successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method.

The chemical compositions of BNNs and as-prepared GQDs/BNNs composite (S9) were characterized by XPS spectra. In Fig. 3 a, the atomic percent contents of B, C, N and O in BNNs are 53.19%, 2.50%, 42.57% and 1.74%, respectively. As expected, the C content in S9 increase to 6.88%, which indicates that GQDs has been effectively supported on BNNs. The peak at 190.7 eV (B–N) and 189.3 eV (B–C–N) shown in Fig. 3b are consistent with the presence of GQDs/BNNs composites [17,18]. Moreover, the other peaks at 192.3 eV origins of the B–O (Fig. 3b) [19–21]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the main graphitic C–C peak at 284.6 eV, the other two peaks at 285.4 and 288.6 eV suggest the existence of C–B and C–N/C–O bonding, respectively [22]. The two peaks at 397.6 eV and 399.1 eV can be attributed to N–B and N–O (Fig. 3d) [23].

According to the TEM images (Fig. 4 a–d), different proportions of GQDs with similar size can be dispersed uniformly on the surface of BNNs. When the proportion of reactants increased to 1:9, a large number of GQDs exist on the surface of BNNs in Fig. 4d. However, when the addition of GQDs continued to increase, the loading has reached saturation. In addition, as for homogeneity, the distribution of B, C and N in S9 can be obtained from Fig. 4e–h. At the same time, the result also proves that GQDs was evenly loaded.

The FT-IR spectra of BNNs, GQDs and S9 in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 are illustrated in Fig. 5 a. The characteristic peaks of B–N–B (∼803 cm−1) and B–N (∼1360 cm−1) belongs to BNNs, and a broad absorption peak from 3000 cm−1 to 3700 cm−1 can be assigned to the vibration of –OH [24]. Furthermore, the peak at 1623 cm−1 is attributed to CC stretching vibration of GQDs [25]. The B–O (∼1023 cm−1), B–C (∼1015 cm−1) and C–N (∼1630 cm−1) are characteristic peaks of GQDs/BNNs, which further illustrates that GQDs has been successfully compounded with BNNs [17]. As shown in Fig. 5b–c, the RL values of GQDs are high in the frequency range of 2.5–12 GHz, showing good microwave absorption performance (RL min = −54.3 dB). However, the value of RL is significantly reduced in the frequency range of 12–17.5 GHz (RL < −10 dB), which cannot meet the basic standard of microwave absorption materials. Therefore, enhancing the microwave absorption performance in the frequency range of 12–17.5 GHz without affecting the strong absorption characteristics in the 2.5–12 GHz range has becomes the key on research.

The RL and attenuation constant (α) values of the samples were calculated basing on Maxwell's equations and transmission line theory. The formulas are listed as follows:

Where μ r

=
ε ′

)
and ε r
(
μ r
=

− j
μ ″
)
are the relative complex permeability and permittivity, ƒ is the frequency of microwave, d is the thickness of absorber, c is the velocity of the electromagnetic wave in vacuum, RL represents reflection loss and Z
in is the normalized input impedance of absorber. μ′, μ″, ε′ and ε″ are the real and imaginary part of the relative permeability and permittivity, respectively [26].

As shown in Fig. 6 a, the value of RL in the region of 2–14 GHz is more than −10 dB, indicating the electromagnetic wave is difficult to be absorbed by pure BNNs which is often used as a traditional EM transmitting material. The RL value of 14–18 GHz starts to increase, and the RL min value is −39.8 dB. Since the microwave absorption is caused by the vibration of B–N bond, when BNNs crystal is excited by microwave, the atoms of B and N vibrate slightly periodically from the equilibrium position in the form of lattice wave, and the absorbed energy is converted into heat. In addition, the wavenumber and energy corresponding to the vibration in the BNNs crystal is calculated to be 0.58 cm−1 and 0.072 meV at the resonance frequency of 17.5 GHz, which match with the calculated phonon dispersion of BNNs [27]. With the addition of GQDs, the bonding form of the system is converted from single B–N to CC, B–C and C–N, resulting in the RL value and absorption frequency have changed. For S1 sample (Fig. 6b), the RL min value increase from −39.8 dB to −63.6 dB. Furthermore, Fig. 6c shows the peak of S3 was significantly enhanced at 3.5–10 GHz and the average absorption intensity reached −20 dB, which clearly indicates that the introduction of GQDs helps to increase the overall absorption frequency and intensity. The RL min value of S5 and S7 samples continued to remain around −60 dB (Fig. 6d and e). Finally, the value of RL min about S9 is −59.9 dB (at 2.8 mm) and the effective absorption frequency range is extended to 2.1–18 GHz in Fig. 6f. Comparing the above data, the addition of BNNs not only does not affect the excellent microwave absorption performance of GQDs in the 2.5–12 GHz band, but also improves the absorption intensity in the 12–17.5 GHz range significantly. Because of its own advantages, h-BN can also greatly improve the thermal conductivity, chemical and high temperature stability of GQDs as well as keeping good microwave absorption performance.

To further discuss the relationship between RL values and microwave performance, ε′, ε″, μ′, μ″, tanδ
E (ε″/ε′) and tanδ
M (μ″/μ′) about BNNs, GQDs and GQDs/BNNs composites are compared in Fig. 7 . Hexagonal BNNs, as a wide bandgap semiconductor, has no good conductivity and lower values of ε′ and ε″ (Fig. 7a and b). With the loading of GQDs with good conductivity, ε′ and ε″ values increase obviously. In addition, μ′ and μ″ values are also relatively low (Fig. 7c and d) because pure BNNs has no magnetic properties. For comparison, ε′ and μ′ of GQDs are slightly higher than those of BNNs, but there is still a certain gap compared with various GQDs/BNNs composites. ε″, μ″, tanδ
E and tanδ
M of GQDs have good values in the frequency region of 2.5–12 GHz, while the values decrease a lot in 12–17.5 GHz. As shown in Fig. 7, S7 and S9 samples have the same good ε″, μ″, tanδ
E and tanδ
M values in the frequency of 2.5–12 and 12–17.5 GHz range. In general, h-BN doped with C atoms can exhibit magnetic semiconductor properties [28]. When GQDs has been loaded, the forming C–N and C–B bonds affects the electron spin of BNNs [16]. For GQDs/BNNs samples, the increase of both μ′ and μ″ as a whole indicates the increase of magnetic loss energy. At the same time, it can be seen from that the loading of GQDs is almost proportional to tanδ
E and tanδ
M values in Fig. 7e and f.

According to Equation (3), α represents the total electrical and magnetic loss of the material. As shown in Fig. 8 a, α values of composites are proportional to the loading amounts of GQDs. In addition, S9 sample has the highest total loss, which indicates that the microwave absorption performance of GQDs/BNNs has been further enhanced. The impedance matching ratio can also be effectively regulated according to the loadings of GQDs in Fig. 8b. The impedance matching ratio of S9 has the highest value among all samples, which is infinitely close to 1. The reason is that the addition of GQDs increases the dielectric constant of materials. Meanwhile, the introduction of C atoms causes BNNs to exhibit ferromagnetism and increase permeability. Thereby, a better matching is achieved between the dielectric constant and permeability.

The microwave absorption properties of BNNs and GQDs/BNNs in high frequency band were investigated at 19–26 and 27–40 GHz (Fig. 9 ). As shown in Fig. 9a, with the increase of GQDs concentration, the RL min values of S1, S3 and S5 samples gradually decrease to about −15 dB in 23.8 GHz. In addition, the RL min values of S7 and S9 samples continued to remain around −17 and −18 dB (Fig. 9a). This result shows that different concentrations of GQDs can adjust the intensity of RL min and absorption frequency. When the electromagnetic field frequency increased to 27–40 GHz, the microwave absorption properties of GQDs/BNNs have obvious regular variety in Fig. 9b. The values of RL min increase gradually from pure BNNs (−6.5 dB) to S9 samples (−13.1 dB).

In comparison with other typical EM absorbing materials in recent references, the microwave absorption performance including RL min and effective bandwidth of various GQDs/BNNs composites are obviously better in Table 1 . The results imply that the synergistic effect of BNNs and GQDs can effectively improve the microwave absorption properties. Firstly, BNNs, as the host material, can make the material unable to be polarized and reducing reflected waves. Secondly, the excellent microwave absorption performance and quantum confinement characteristics of GQDs can be used to absorb electromagnetic wave energy to the greatest extent.

According to the classical electrodynamics principle, microwaves do not consume the energy when propagating in a vacuum or an ideal insulating medium. Due to the presence of α, the high-frequency electromagnetic wave is polarized and magnetized after contacting the conductor. The conductive current and induced magnetic field are generated on the surface of the conductor to absorb the energy of the external electromagnetic field. This part of the absorbed energy must satisfy the Planck-Einstein formula.

Based on the light absorption theory of solids and quantum electrodynamics, the electromagnetic field is a collection of photons, and absorption process of GQDs under the action of electromagnetic waves is the photonic absorption process of free carriers. As shown in Fig. 10 , when electromagnetic waves are applied to GQDs, the electrons in GQDs absorb photon energy, which leads to the transition of electrons from the ground state energy levels to the excited state energy levels [37]. The absorption process of free carriers is the transition between the electronic states in the same band. Because the free carrier absorption of photons must conform to the energy law and cannot occur infinitely, it will be accompanied by phonon scattering and ionization of impurities. At the same time, carrier absorption is closely related to carrier concentration. The higher the carrier concentration, the higher the absorption coefficient. Therefore, with the increase of GQDs loading, the carrier concentration will be increased and the microwave absorption performance of the composites will be improved [38,39]. Various chemical bonds such as CC, B–C, C–N and B–N bonds are formed on the surface of BNNs, which consequently allows the absorption region to expand from a single GQDs region to the surface of whole composites. The result indicates simultaneously increases the absorption region and total absorption properties of GQDs/BNNs.

In summary, for the first time, GQDs was employed to prepare GQDs/BNNs composites as microwave absorbent. The composites exhibited microwave absorption properties at 2–18 GHz bands with an RL min value as low as −59.9 dB at 2.8 mm. At the same time, BNNs can significantly improve the microwave absorption performance of GQDs in the high frequency range of 12–17.5 GHz. When the frequency band further increases to 19–27 and 28–40 GHz, the values of RL min are also less than −10 dB, which shows that GQDs/BNNs can be used as excellent absorbent in broadband. In addition, the composites can have good thermal conductivity, chemical and high temperature stability. Therefore, our work illustrated the great potential of GQDs and BN based composites in the microwave absorption.

Hu Qi: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Investigation; Yi Fang: Supervision, Project administration, Resources; Zhao Du: Formal analysis, Visualization; Zhonglu Guo: Formal analysis; Zhenya Liu: Formal analysis; Yang Huang: Formal analysis; Jing Lin: Formal analysis; Chengchun Tang: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51772075), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2018202129) and Key Program of Hebei Province Higher Education Science and Technology Research Foundation (ZD2018082).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced microwave absorption performance from magnetic coupling of magnetic nanoparticles suspended within hierarchically tubular composite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.C. Wu, K. Pei, L.S. Xing, X.F. Yu, W.B. You, R.C. Che, Enhanced microwave absorption performance from magnetic coupling of magnetic nanoparticles suspended within hierarchically tubular composite, Adv. Funct. Mater. 29 (2019) 1901448.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel hierarchical RGO/MoS2/K-aMnO2 composite architectures with enhanced broadband microwave absorption performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Hu, Y. Fang, J.J. Wang, Z. Du, Q.Q. Song, Z.L. Guo, et al., Novel hierarchical RGO/MoS2/K-aMnO2 composite architectures with enhanced broadband microwave absorption performance, J. Mater. Chem. C 7 (2019) 13878-13886.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boosted interfacial polarization from multishell TiO2@Fe3O4@PPy heterojunction for enhanced microwave absorption

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.J. Ding, L. Wang, Y.H. Zhao, L.S. Xing, X.F. Yu, G.Y. Chen, et al., Boosted interfacial polarization from multishell TiO2@Fe3O4@PPy heterojunction for enhanced microwave absorption, Small 15 (2019) 1902885.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controllable synthesis of γ-Fe2O3 nanotube/porous rGO composites and their enhanced microwave absorption properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Wang, Q.Z. Jiao, X.F. Liu, Y.C. Xu, Q. Shi, S. Yue, et al., Controllable synthesis of γ-Fe2O3 nanotube/porous rGO composites and their enhanced microwave absorption properties, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng.. 7 (2019) 7004-7013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave absorption enhancement and complex permittivity and permeability of Fe encapsulated within carbon nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.C. Che, L.M. Peng, X.F. Duan, Q. Chen, X.L. Liang, Microwave absorption enhancement and complex permittivity and permeability of Fe encapsulated within carbon nanotubes, Adv. Mater. 16 (2004) 401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced microwave absorption by arrayed carbon fibers and gradient dispersion of Fe nanoparticles in epoxy resin composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Shah, A. Ding, Y.H. Wang, L. Zhang, D.X. Wang, J. Muhammad, et al., Enhanced microwave absorption by arrayed carbon fibers and gradient dispersion of Fe nanoparticles in epoxy resin composites, Carbon 96 (2016) 987-997.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic aligned Fe3O4-reduced graphene oxide/waterborne polyurethane composites with controllable structure for high microwaves absorption capacity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.W. Dai, Y.H. Zhai, L. Wu, Y. Zhang, Magnetic aligned Fe3O4-reduced graphene oxide/waterborne polyurethane composites with controllable structure for high microwaves absorption capacity, Carbon 152 (2019) 661-670.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Confinedly implanted NiFe2O4-rGO: cluster tailoring and highly tunable electromagnetic properties for selective-frequency microwave absorption

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.L. Zhang, X.X. Wang, M.S. Cao, Confinedly implanted NiFe2O4-rGO: cluster tailoring and highly tunable electromagnetic properties for selective-frequency microwave absorption, Nano Res. 11 (2018) 1426-1436.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetics and spintronics on two-dimensional composite materials of graphene/hexagonal boron nitride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.G. Wang, X.F. Xu, X.J. Mu, F.C. Ma, M.T. Sun, Magnetics and spintronics on two-dimensional composite materials of graphene/hexagonal boron nitride, Mater. Today Phys. 3 (2017) 93-117.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and study on microwave absorbing materials of boron nitride coated pyrolytic carbon particles
